metadata.name: Gunny Olympic Weightlifting Corpus
metadata.scope: Olympic weightlifting only — Snatch and Clean & Jerk. Does not cover powerlifting, strongman, or general strength.
metadata.depth: Layered. Every concept node carries an athlete_view (cues, feel, what to do) and a coach_view (biomechanics, technical priorities, why it matters).

node_schema.title: string — human-readable name
node_schema.summary: string — one-line description for retrieval ranking

metadata.rules_reference: IWF Technical and Competition Rules & Regulations (TCRR). USAW follows IWF for sanctioned events. Local meets may vary on equipment specs but lifting rules are uniform.

authorities.name: Greg Everett
authorities.source: Catalyst Athletics / 'Olympic Weightlifting: A Complete Guide for Athletes & Coaches'

authorities.name: Glenn Pendlay
authorities.domain: technique, the Pendlay row, classical American coaching

authorities.name: A.S. Medvedev
authorities.source: 'A System of Multi-Year Training in Weightlifting'

metadata.voice_notes_for_gunny: When delivering this content, Gunny stays in DI register. Athlete_view content is what gets spoken to operators by default — short, declarative, action-oriented. Coach_view is for technical questions, programming discussions, or when an operator asks 'why'. Common_errors and drills are pulled when an operator describes a problem or asks for correctives. Cite authorities by name when challenged or when an operator asks for sources, not by default.

snatch.title: The Snatch
snatch.summary: One-motion lift from floor to overhead in a full squat. Most technical lift in weightlifting.

setup.title: Snatch Setup
setup.summary: Static start position before the bar leaves the floor. Sets every leverage downstream.

foot_position.title: Foot Position at Setup
foot_position.summary: Feet roughly hip-width, toes slightly out. Bar over mid-foot.

athlete_view.feel: Stable. Like you're about to jump straight up.
key_actions: Pull stance is narrower than your squat stance
Bar should be over the bony bumps at the base of your big toe, not the middle of the foot

coach_view.biomechanics: Hip-width stance optimizes hip extension torque while keeping the bar path close to the body's center of mass. Toe-out 7-15 degrees lets the knees track outside the elbows during the receive in the bottom of the squat.
priorities: Bar over mid-foot to base-of-toes
Stance narrow enough to allow aggressive triple extension
Stance wide enough to receive in a deep squat
coach_view.why_it_matters: If the bar starts forward of mid-foot, the lifter must pull it back during the first pull, costing vertical force and creating a forward bar path. If feet are too narrow, knees collide with elbows in the catch.

common_errors.error: Stance too wide (squat width)
common_errors.fix: Narrow it. Pull stance is hip-width. Squat stance is for the catch only — feet jump out during the turnover.

common_errors.error: Bar too far forward at setup
common_errors.fix: Roll the bar back until it's over the base of the toes / mid-foot. Re-set.

drills: Setup-and-pause drill: assume position, hold 3 seconds, check bar position
Tape a line on the platform under mid-foot for visual reference

grip_width.title: Snatch Grip Width
grip_width.summary: Wide enough that the bar sits at the hip crease when standing tall, hook grip mandatory.

cues: Bar in the hip crease standing tall
Wrap the thumb hard, then bury it
athlete_view.feel: Pinch on the thumb. Uncomfortable for the first month — get over it.
key_actions: Stand with the bar at arms length and slide hands out until bar contacts the hip crease
Lock thumb against the bar with index and middle finger over the thumbnail

coach_view.biomechanics: Wider grip shortens the distance the bar must travel to reach overhead and improves overhead stability via reduced shoulder flexion demand. The hook grip uses bone-on-bone friction (thumb against bar) so the fingers don't have to grip-strength their way through max efforts.
priorities: Grip width set by anatomy (longer arms = wider grip)
Hook grip non-negotiable above ~70% intensity
Tape the thumbs once skin tolerance becomes the limiter
coach_view.why_it_matters: Lifters who fail the hook grip on heavy snatches almost always lose the bar forward in the third pull because the wrists must grip-clench instead of relaxing into the turnover.

common_errors.error: Grip too narrow — bar lands above the hip crease
common_errors.fix: Move out one-to-two finger widths. Test by standing tall — bar should sit in the crease, not on the abs.

common_errors.error: Refusing to hook grip
common_errors.fix: Non-negotiable. Tape the thumbs, hook every warmup, build tolerance. There is no elite snatch without hook grip.

drills: Hook grip deadlifts at 60-70% to build skin tolerance
Snatch grip from blocks with extended holds at top

back_and_hip_position.title: Back Angle and Hip Height at Setup
back_and_hip_position.summary: Chest up, back arched, hips above knees but below shoulders, shoulders slightly forward of the bar.

athlete_view.feel: Loaded into the hamstrings and glutes. Not squatting the bar up — hinging.
key_actions: Take a big breath, brace the trunk, lock the lats by trying to bend the bar around your shins
Lats engage by pulling the shoulders down and back

coach_view.biomechanics: Optimal start position varies by anthropometry. Long-femur lifters set up with higher hips and more horizontal back; short-femur lifters can set up with lower hips and more vertical torso. The shoulders sit slightly forward of the bar (1-3 inches) so the bar travels backward into the body during the second pull, not away from it.
priorities: Lumbar neutral or slightly extended, never flexed
Lats engaged before liftoff
Shoulder-bar relationship matched to femur length
coach_view.why_it_matters: Hips too low = the lift becomes a squat, knees push forward, bar swings out. Hips too high = stiff-leg deadlift, no leg drive, slow first pull. Lat engagement is the single most-coached and most-missed setup detail.

common_errors.error: Lumbar flexion (rounded lower back)
common_errors.fix: Stop loading more weight until the position is solid. Pause snatch deadlifts at knee. Strengthen erectors with good mornings and back extensions.

common_errors.error: Hips too low — squatting the start
common_errors.fix: Set hips above the knee crease. Cue 'hinge, don't squat'.

common_errors.error: Shoulders behind the bar
common_errors.fix: Bar over mid-foot, shoulders slightly forward. Otherwise the first pull launches the bar away from the body.

drills: Halting snatch deadlifts with 2-second pause at knee
Snatch from a setup hold (3-second static start)

first_pull.title: First Pull (Floor to Knee)
first_pull.summary: Slow, patient, leg-driven. Shoulders stay over the bar. Sets up the second pull.

tempo_and_intent.title: Tempo and Intent of the First Pull
tempo_and_intent.summary: Slow relative to the second pull — but accelerating. 'Patient pull' is the standard cue.

athlete_view.feel: Like a deadlift with intent. Heavy. Smooth.
key_actions: Initiate by pressing legs into the platform, not by pulling the bar with the back
Bar accelerates continuously — there is no static moment until lockout

coach_view.biomechanics: First-pull bar velocity in elite lifters is roughly 0.8-1.2 m/s at the knee. The pull is leg-driven (knee extension) with a constant or slightly opening hip-shoulder angle. The back angle stays approximately constant from floor to knee.
priorities: Constant back angle floor-to-knee
Knee extension drives the pull, not back extension
Bar accelerates — no plateau
coach_view.why_it_matters: If the lifter rips the bar off the floor, the back angle changes early (hips shoot up), removing leg drive from the second pull. The first pull is patient specifically so that the second pull can be violent.

common_errors.error: Yanking the bar off the floor
common_errors.fix: Pause-pull at 1 inch off the floor for 2 seconds, then continue. Re-trains the patient initiation.

common_errors.error: Hips shoot up — shoulders rise, hips rise, bar barely moves
common_errors.fix: Cue 'shoulders and hips rise together'. Snatch pulls with chains/bands to demand leg drive.

drills: Snatch from a deficit (1-2 inch riser) to enforce leg drive
Tempo snatch deadlift (3-second eccentric to floor, 2-second concentric to knee)

bar_path_first_pull.title: Bar Path in the First Pull
bar_path_first_pull.summary: Bar travels backward and slightly upward — toward the lifter, never away.

athlete_view.feel: Like the bar is dragging up the shins, then up the thighs.
key_actions: Engage lats hard at setup so the bar doesn't drift forward
Bar should brush or just barely clear the shins

coach_view.biomechanics: The bar moves toward the lifter from the floor to the knee — a backward horizontal displacement of roughly 5-10cm in elite lifters. This puts the bar over the mid-foot or slightly behind it as the knee approaches extension, which is the optimal position for the second pull's vertical drive.
priorities: Lat engagement before liftoff
Bar contacts or brushes the shins
No forward bar drift in the first pull
coach_view.why_it_matters: Forward bar drift in the first pull is the single most common amateur error. It puts the bar in front of the body for the second pull, causing the lifter to either jump forward or loop the bar away. Both kill the lift.

common_errors.error: Bar swings out and away from shins
common_errors.fix: Drill lat engagement: 'bend the bar around your shins'. Use a snatch deadlift with PVC against the shins.

common_errors.error: Bar drifts forward as it passes the knee
common_errors.fix: Pause snatch at knee. Hold for 2 seconds. Re-engage lats. Continue.

drills: Snatch pulls vs the wall (shins 1 inch from wall — bar must stay close)
Snatch deadlift with PVC pressed against the shins

transition.title: Transition (Knee to Power Position)
transition.summary: Knee re-bends, torso starts to come vertical, bar contacts upper thigh / hip crease. The 'scoop' or 'double knee bend.'

double_knee_bend.title: Double Knee Bend (the Scoop)
double_knee_bend.summary: After the bar passes the knee, the knees re-bend slightly forward as the torso comes vertical. Loads the legs for the second pull.

cues: Get the bar in the pocket
athlete_view.feel: A loaded spring. Quad pre-tension. Bar grazing the upper thigh.
key_actions: As the bar passes the knee, drive the knees slightly back under the bar
Torso continues to come vertical — but smoothly, not aggressively

coach_view.biomechanics: The double knee bend re-loads the quadriceps and patellar tendon for the explosive triple extension of the second pull. Knee re-flexion of 10-20 degrees is typical. The bar reaches mid-to-upper thigh and continues to upper-thigh/hip-crease contact point depending on grip width and limb proportions.
priorities: Smooth knee re-bend, not a deliberate drop
Bar stays close — contact at upper thigh
Torso vertical at the contact point
coach_view.why_it_matters: Without the double knee bend, the lifter has no leg drive available at the contact point — the lift becomes an arms-and-back rip. With it, the second pull is a vertical jump with a barbell.

common_errors.error: Skipping the scoop — straight legs at the contact point
common_errors.fix: Tall snatches and high-hang snatches force the lifter to re-bend the knees to find leg drive.

common_errors.error: Bar contact at the wrong height (mid-thigh, not hip crease)
common_errors.fix: Adjust grip width or pull mechanics. Wider grip drops the contact point lower; narrower raises it.

drills: Tall snatch (start at standing, dip and snatch)
Pause-at-knee snatch to feel the scoop separately

second_pull.title: Second Pull (Explosive Extension)
second_pull.summary: Triple extension — ankle, knee, hip — driven vertically. The 'violence' of the lift. Bar contacts hip crease, then accelerates upward.

triple_extension.title: Triple Extension
triple_extension.summary: Simultaneous, aggressive extension of ankle, knee, and hip joints, driving the bar vertically off the hip.

cues: Drive through the heels and explode
athlete_view.feel: Like a max vertical jump while holding the bar.
key_actions: Aggressive hip drive at contact
Stand fully tall before pulling under
Heels stay down as long as possible — extend the ankles last

coach_view.biomechanics: The second pull generates the highest power output of any lift in sport — peak power outputs of 2,000-5,000W are common in competitive lifters. Triple extension creates vertical bar acceleration that gives the lifter time to pull under. Hip extension contributes the most force; ankle extension (plantar flexion) contributes the least but completes the chain.
priorities: Vertical force vector — no forward lean at extension
Hip drive into the bar, not jumping forward
Full extension before initiating the third pull
coach_view.why_it_matters: Cutting the second pull short ('soft pull') means the bar has insufficient upward momentum, and the lifter must rush the turnover with the arms — the bar crashes down on a weak overhead position.

common_errors.error: Jumping forward instead of vertical
common_errors.fix: Snatch on a low platform with markers — feet should land in the same spot or slightly behind. Cue 'finish stacked.'

common_errors.error: Cutting the pull short — pulling under before full extension
common_errors.fix: Snatch high pulls forced to full extension. Tall snatches. Cue 'finish, then pull under.'

common_errors.error: Early arm bend — biceps activate during extension
common_errors.fix: Snatch pulls at 100-110% snatch with strict 'arms long' cue. Penalize early arm bend with a re-set.

drills: Snatch high pull with 3-second hold at top

bar_contact_point.title: Bar Contact at the Hip
bar_contact_point.summary: Bar makes brushing contact with the hip crease. Not a slap, not a smash — a brush that redirects momentum vertically.

athlete_view.feel: A whisper of contact, then the bar shoots up.
key_actions: Drive hips forward into the bar, not the bar into the hips
Maintain bar-body contact through extension

coach_view.biomechanics: Contact at the hip crease (PSIS / inguinal area) maximizes vertical force transfer. A slapping contact suggests the bar was forward of the body and crashed into it — energy is wasted in the horizontal direction. A bar-too-low contact (mid-thigh) means the lifter ran out of pull height and the second pull was rushed.
priorities: Brushing contact, not impact
Contact point at hip crease for snatch grip
Hips drive into the bar
coach_view.why_it_matters: The contact point is a diagnostic. Bruised hips mean the bar is crashing in (forward bar path). No contact at all means the bar swung out. The brush is the goal.

common_errors.error: Bar smashes the hips (bruising)
common_errors.fix: First-pull bar path correction — get the bar over mid-foot earlier. Snatch pulls with focus on bar staying close.

common_errors.error: Bar contacts mid-thigh or below
common_errors.fix: Grip is too narrow, or the lifter is cutting the pull short. Widen grip; drill tall snatches.

drills: Snatch from the pocket / power position

turnover.title: Turnover / Third Pull (Pulling Under the Bar)
turnover.summary: The lifter pulls themselves under a bar that is already accelerating upward. Elbows pull high and outside, then rotate around the bar to lockout overhead.

pull_under_intent.title: Pulling Under, Not Pulling Up
pull_under_intent.summary: The third pull moves the lifter down, not the bar up. The bar's upward momentum is gifted by the second pull.

athlete_view.feel: Like falling into a squat with the bar suspended above you.
key_actions: Pull elbows high and to the sides (not back)
Rotate elbows around the bar at the top — fast and aggressive
Punch up into lockout while dropping into the squat

coach_view.biomechanics: At peak bar height (peak velocity ~1.8-2.2 m/s vertical for elite snatches), the bar decelerates under gravity. The lifter must beat the bar to the receive position. Elite turnover times (peak extension to bar lockout overhead) are 0.15-0.25 seconds.
priorities: Aggressive elbow path — high and outside
Active wrist rotation
Speed under matches speed up
coach_view.why_it_matters: A slow turnover means the lifter catches the bar after it has fallen below the receive position — the bar crashes down, the catch position collapses. Speed under is a trainable skill, not a strength quality.

common_errors.error: Pulling the bar up with the arms instead of pulling under
common_errors.fix: Tall snatch. Drop snatch. Both train the pull-under without rewarding the pull-up.

common_errors.error: Slow turnover — bar crashes down on top of lifter
common_errors.fix: Speed snatches at 60-70%. Many sets, low reps, max speed. Drop snatches.

drills: Speed snatch (60-70%, focus on turnover speed)

receive.title: Receive Position (Overhead Squat Catch)
receive.summary: Bar locked overhead, in a full squat below parallel. Active shoulders, locked elbows, midline solid.

overhead_position.title: Overhead Position in the Catch
overhead_position.summary: Bar is slightly behind the ears, elbows locked, shoulders shrugged into the ears, scapulae upward-rotated.

cues: Bar over the back of your neck
athlete_view.feel: Like you're trying to push the bar through the ceiling.
key_actions: Active shrug — push the shoulders up into the bar
Lock elbows hard
Bar slightly behind the crown of the head

coach_view.biomechanics: Stable overhead requires (1) full shoulder flexion with external rotation, (2) thoracic extension, (3) active scapular upward rotation, and (4) wrist extension. The bar's vertical line of force must pass through the midfoot for the squat to be stable. Behind-the-ear position is not a 'press behind the neck' — it's where the bar naturally settles when the scapulae are properly positioned.
priorities: Active shoulder packing
Elbow lockout — never soft
Bar over mid-foot in the squat
coach_view.why_it_matters: A passive overhead position collapses under load. Soft elbows are an immediate red light. Bar too far forward or back puts the lifter in a battle to stand up.

common_errors.error: Soft elbows in the catch
common_errors.fix: Snatch balance. Overhead squats with 3-second hold at bottom. Cue 'punch the bar through the ceiling.'

common_errors.error: Bar caught too far forward (above face/eyes)
common_errors.fix: Improves with thoracic mobility and active shrug. Behind-the-neck press complex; sots press for active end-range.

common_errors.error: Bar caught too far behind, lifter falls forward
common_errors.fix: Address shoulder mobility. Often a setup or pull-path issue, not a catch issue — the bar was already too far back.

depth_and_squat.title: Squat Depth in the Receive
depth_and_squat.summary: Hip crease below knee crease. IWF rules require the lifter to stand from the depth they catch in.

cues: Bounce out of the hole if you can
athlete_view.feel: Like a fast overhead squat to full depth.
key_actions: Catch in the deepest position your mobility allows
Use the eccentric-to-concentric reflex to stand up — don't pause

coach_view.biomechanics: Catching deep is an efficiency play. Lower catch height = more bar height available before the lift fails = more allowable error in the second pull. Power snatches (catching at or above parallel) are training tools, not the goal of the full snatch. Standing from depth requires a strong overhead squat — programmed separately.
priorities: Catch as deep as the bar allows
No 'bouncing' if it compromises position
Overhead squat strength supports catch depth
coach_view.why_it_matters: A lifter who can only catch at parallel needs to pull the bar 4-6 inches higher than a lifter who catches in the hole. Same percentage of max, more time, more risk.

common_errors.error: Catching high (parallel or above)
common_errors.fix: Drop snatches. Snatch balance from full depth. Improves over months, not weeks.

common_errors.error: Failing to stand from the bottom
common_errors.fix: Overhead squat strength. Pause overhead squats. Front squats and back squats for total leg strength.

drills: Snatch balance with 2-second hold at bottom

recovery.title: Recovery (Stand from the Squat)
recovery.summary: Stand from the bottom of the squat with bar overhead. Maintain bar position throughout.

stand_from_squat.title: Standing from the Snatch
stand_from_squat.summary: Drive out of the bottom while keeping bar locked, active, and over mid-foot.

cues: Push the bar up while standing up
athlete_view.feel: Heavy overhead squat. Concentrate on the bar position.
key_actions: Initiate stand by driving knees out and pressing through midfoot
Keep actively pressing the bar up the entire stand
Once standing, hold the lift until the down signal — three white lights

coach_view.biomechanics: Failure to stand is rarely overhead failure — it's leg strength failure. If the lifter catches deep with a stable overhead, the stand requires only the lifter's overhead squat capacity. Overhead squat 1RM should typically be 100-105% of snatch 1RM for elite lifters.
priorities: Knees track over toes — knees out
Continuous pressure into the bar overhead
Hold the lift to the down signal
coach_view.why_it_matters: Standing too quickly often jolts the bar forward or back; standing too slowly burns energy. Smooth stand, then hold.

common_errors.error: Bar drifts forward as lifter stands
common_errors.fix: Lat tension overhead. Pause overhead squats with focus on bar position. Address thoracic mobility if it's a positional limit.

common_errors.error: Lifter drops the bar before the down signal
common_errors.fix: Coach call: 'wait for the lights.' Drill standing-and-holding for 3-5 seconds in training.

drills: Pause overhead squat (3-second pause at bottom)

errors_overview.title: Top Snatch Failure Modes
errors_overview.summary: The most common ways snatches die, ranked.

ranked_failures.root_cause: Forward bar drift in the first pull or jumping forward in the second pull
ranked_failures.fix_path: Diagnose first-pull bar path → snatch pulls with lat focus → tempo first pulls

ranked_failures.root_cause: Pulling the bar up instead of pulling under
ranked_failures.fix_path: Tall snatches, drop snatches, speed snatches at 60-70%

ranked_failures.root_cause: Weak overhead, slow lockout, or under-extending the second pull
ranked_failures.fix_path: Snatch balance, behind-the-neck snatch press, overhead squat

ranked_failures.root_cause: Insufficient bar height from second pull
ranked_failures.fix_path: Snatch high pulls at 100-110% snatch, attack hip drive, build leg strength

ranked_failures.root_cause: Insufficient overhead squat strength or unstable catch position
ranked_failures.fix_path: Pause overhead squats, snatch balance, front and back squat strength work

variants.title: Snatch Variations
variants.summary: Common variants and their training purpose.

variants.name: Power Snatch
variants.description: Catch above parallel. Trains pull height, second pull power.
variants.use: Speed-strength block, novice progression, metcon work.

variants.name: Hang Snatch (Knee / Above-Knee / High-Hang)
variants.description: Start from a hanging position above the floor.
variants.use: Isolates second pull / scoop. Good for technique and assistance volume.

variants.name: Block Snatch
variants.description: Bar starts on blocks at various heights.
variants.use: Isolates phases. Removes first-pull volume to spare lower back.

variants.name: Snatch Balance
variants.description: Bar on back, dip-drive, drop into overhead squat with bar locked.

variants.name: Drop Snatch
variants.description: Bar on back, no dip-drive — drop straight under.
variants.use: Pure pull-under speed. No leg drive component.

variants.name: Tall Snatch
variants.description: Start standing on tip-toes, bar at hips, no dip — drop under.
variants.use: Forces extension before pull-under. Trains turnover speed.

variants.name: Muscle Snatch
variants.description: No re-bend of knees in receive — pull bar overhead in standing.
variants.use: Pulling strength, turnover. Loads stay light.

clean_and_jerk.title: The Clean & Jerk
clean_and_jerk.summary: Two-part lift. Clean: floor to front-rack receive in a squat. Jerk: rack to overhead via dip-drive-split (or squat).

clean.title: The Clean
clean.summary: Floor-to-front-rack pull. Same first/second pull pattern as snatch with narrower grip and higher catch position.

setup.title: Clean Setup
setup.summary: Same principles as snatch setup, with a narrower grip — typically just outside the hips.

grip_width.title: Clean Grip Width
grip_width.summary: Hands just outside the hips. Hook grip mandatory. Grip width sized so the elbows clear the knees in the receive.

cues: Elbows clear the knees in the catch
athlete_view.feel: Tighter grip than snatch, but the bar still hits the same hip crease.
key_actions: Test grip by performing front squat — elbows should not collide with knees
Hook grip for the pull, can be released in the front rack

coach_view.biomechanics: Narrow grip means the bar contacts higher on the body during the second pull (around the upper thigh / hip). Front rack receives the bar on the shoulders/clavicle, not the hands — the hands are merely a guide.
priorities: Grip width that allows elbow clearance in the catch
Hook grip for the pull
Front rack mobility independent of grip width
coach_view.why_it_matters: A clean grip too narrow forces the elbows out wide in the catch and racks the bar on the wrists, causing wrist strain. Too wide and the bar bounces off the chest in the receive.

common_errors.error: Grip too narrow — elbows can't clear knees in catch
common_errors.fix: Move out 1-2 finger widths.

common_errors.error: Wrist pain in the front rack
common_errors.fix: Improve front-rack flexibility — wrist, lat, triceps. Foam roll lats. Loosen grip in the receive.

drills: Front squat with proper rack hold

pull_phases.title: Clean Pull (First, Transition, Second)
pull_phases.summary: Mechanics mirror the snatch pull. Slower first pull, scoop, explosive second pull.

shared_mechanics.title: What Carries Over from the Snatch
shared_mechanics.summary: Pull tempo, bar path, double knee bend, triple extension — all identical in principle to the snatch.

cues: Same vertical drive — bar to the hip
athlete_view.feel: Heavier first pull (you can lift more clean than snatch), but the rhythm is the same.
key_actions: Apply all snatch first/second pull cues to the clean
Bar contact point is slightly higher (mid-to-upper thigh) due to narrower grip

coach_view.biomechanics: Clean second pull velocity is lower than snatch (by ~10-15%) because the load is heavier and the catch position is lower (front rack, not overhead). The transition from second pull to receive is faster in absolute terms because there's less bar height to clear.
priorities: Don't 'muscle' the clean because you're stronger — same patient first pull
Hip drive is everything — narrower grip means less arm contribution
coach_view.why_it_matters: Lifters who treat the clean as 'a deadlift you flip' instead of 'a snatch with closer hands' miss cleans because they yank the bar.

common_errors.error: Pulling cleans with the back instead of the legs
common_errors.fix: Same fix as snatch — pause first pulls, tempo deadlifts, leg-drive cues.

common_errors.error: Bar path forward in second pull (bar swings out)
common_errors.fix: Snatch-grip pulls (yes, even for cleans) drill the lat tension; transfer to clean pulls.

drills: See snatch.first_pull and snatch.second_pull drills — apply to clean grip

rack_receive.title: Front Rack Receive
rack_receive.summary: Bar lands on the shoulders/clavicle in a deep front squat. Elbows high, fingers under the bar (not gripping).

rack_position.title: The Front Rack
rack_position.summary: Bar resting on shoulders/anterior delts, elbows pointed forward and slightly up, hands relaxed.

cues: Bar on the shoulders, not the hands
Punch the elbows forward in the catch
athlete_view.feel: Bar is being held by the shoulders. Hands could let go if needed.
key_actions: Drive elbows forward and up as the bar arrives
Open the hand if needed — fingers under, palms up
Stay tight in the front squat

coach_view.biomechanics: The rack position depends on shoulder mobility (flexion + external rotation), wrist extension, and lat length. The fingers do not support the bar — the deltoid shelf does. Inability to rack is almost always a lat or thoracic mobility issue.
priorities: Elbows high — parallel to floor or higher
Bar on shelf, not on hands
Tight upper back during the front squat stand
coach_view.why_it_matters: A poor rack collapses under heavy weight. The bar rolls forward, lifter dumps the lift. A high rack lets the lifter front squat heavy without compromising structure.

common_errors.error: Elbows drop in the catch
common_errors.fix: Front squat 5x3 with 2-second pause — train the rack under load. Tall cleans for catch position drill.

common_errors.error: Bar rests on the wrists, causing pain
common_errors.fix: Loosen the grip in the catch. Improve lat and triceps mobility.

drills: Front squat with pause at bottom
Clean grip front rack hold (just rack, hold for time)

stand_from_clean.title: Standing from the Clean
stand_from_clean.summary: Front squat out of the catch. Maintain rack, elbows up, midline locked.

cues: Big breath at bottom, hold it on the way up
athlete_view.feel: Heavy front squat. Reset breath at top before the jerk.
key_actions: Brace, drive knees out, stand
Do not start the jerk until you've reset breath and footing

coach_view.biomechanics: Front squat 1RM should be ~110-115% of clean 1RM for elite lifters — more for some, but never less. The recovery from the clean is a heavy front squat without a fresh setup.
priorities: Elbows up through the entire stand
Knees track out
Reset breathing for the jerk
coach_view.why_it_matters: Cleaners who barely stand have nothing left for the jerk. The clean must feel manageable for the jerk to succeed.

common_errors.error: Elbows collapse on the stand-up
common_errors.fix: Front squat strength. Pause front squats. Increase rack mobility.

common_errors.error: Rushing into the jerk while still gassed
common_errors.fix: Coach call: 'reset.' Breathe, set feet, then jerk.

jerk.title: The Jerk
jerk.summary: Drive the bar from front rack to overhead via vertical dip-drive and split (or squat) catch.

rack_and_setup.title: Jerk Setup (Rack and Foot Position)
rack_and_setup.summary: Bar resettles on the shelf with elbows slightly lower than clean rack. Feet under hips, weight balanced midfoot.

elbow_position_for_jerk.title: Elbow Position for the Jerk
elbow_position_for_jerk.summary: Slightly lower than the clean catch — elbows about 30-45 degrees below horizontal. Bar shelved on shoulders, hand contact light.

athlete_view.feel: Bar is locked into the body. Stable.
key_actions: Drop elbows from the clean rack
Take a big breath into the belly and brace
Eyes on the horizon — head neutral

coach_view.biomechanics: Elbow angle for the jerk is a tradeoff. Too high (clean rack) and the bar can't transfer cleanly to overhead — the elbows must rotate during the drive. Too low and the bar slides forward off the shelf during the dip. The 30-45 degree range balances both.
priorities: Bar on the shelf, not the hands
Stable rack — bar shouldn't move during the dip
Vertical posture — chest tall
coach_view.why_it_matters: If the bar slides forward in the dip, the drive launches forward. The whole jerk fails before the drive even starts.

common_errors.error: Elbows too high — bar can't drive cleanly off shelf
common_errors.fix: Drop elbows after the clean is racked. Practice in jerk drives from rack.

common_errors.error: Elbows too low — bar rolls forward in dip
common_errors.fix: Raise elbows to ~30-45 degrees below horizontal. Brace tightly.

drills: Jerk drives from rack (no overhead — just dip and drive)

dip.title: The Dip
dip.summary: Vertical, controlled, ~10% of body height. Knees bend, weight stays mid-foot, torso stays vertical.

vertical_dip.title: Vertical Dip
vertical_dip.summary: Knees track out, hips drop straight down, torso stays vertical, weight midfoot.

athlete_view.feel: Like sitting straight down between your feet.
key_actions: Slow on the way down (1-2 seconds), fast change of direction, fast on the way up
Heels stay down through the dip

coach_view.biomechanics: Dip depth is typically 10-12% of standing height (around 4-6 inches for most lifters). The dip stretches the quadriceps and patellar tendon for the drive's stretch-shortening cycle. Vertical torso is non-negotiable — any forward lean translates to a forward bar path on the drive.
priorities: Vertical torso through the dip
Slow eccentric, fast change of direction
Weight stays mid-foot — no forward shift
coach_view.why_it_matters: Forward lean in the dip is the most common jerk killer. The bar drives forward, lifter chases it, lockout fails. Vertical dip → vertical drive → vertical bar → solid lockout.

common_errors.error: Forward torso lean in the dip
common_errors.fix: Pause jerk dips against a wall — back stays in contact. Cue 'hips between your feet.'

common_errors.error: Dipping too fast — losing tightness
common_errors.fix: Tempo dips (3-second descent, 0 pause, fast drive). Builds bracing under load.

common_errors.error: Heels rising in the dip
common_errors.fix: Cue weight in heels and mid-foot. Strengthen quadriceps with paused front squats.

drills: Pause jerk dip (3-second hold at bottom of dip)

drive.title: The Drive
drive.summary: Aggressive vertical leg extension launches the bar off the shoulders. Arms come into play only after legs finish.

leg_drive_first.title: Legs Drive, Arms Finish
leg_drive_first.summary: The drive is a vertical jump with the bar on the shoulders. Arms lock out only after the bar separates from the shoulders.

cues: Bar leaves the shoulders before the arms move
athlete_view.feel: Like jumping straight up with a heavy bar in front rack.
key_actions: Aggressive vertical drive through the heels and mid-foot
Bar leaves the shoulders from leg drive alone
Arms lock out aggressively only at the top

coach_view.biomechanics: The jerk drive generates 1.5-2x bodyweight in vertical force at peak in elite lifters. Arms contribute almost nothing to the drive itself — they are a passive transmission until the bar separates from the shoulders, at which point they aggressively lock out. Premature arm action ('press jerk') leaks force horizontally and short-circuits the drive.
priorities: Vertical leg drive
Arms passive until shoulder separation
Aggressive lockout once bar is airborne
coach_view.why_it_matters: Pressing the jerk is the second most common failure (after forward dip). Once arms engage during the drive, leg power can no longer be transmitted to the bar.

common_errors.error: Pressing the jerk (arms early)
common_errors.fix: Push press for arm-leg sequencing. Jerk drives without overhead lockout — feel the leg drive in isolation.

common_errors.error: Jumping forward in the drive
common_errors.fix: Vertical dip first. Jerks from blocks or pins to enforce verticality.

split_receive.title: Split Jerk Receive
split_receive.summary: Front foot lands flat, back foot on ball. Front knee bent ~120 degrees, torso vertical, bar locked overhead.

split_position.title: The Split Position
split_position.summary: Stable, vertical, balanced. Roughly 60% weight on front foot, 40% on back foot.

cues: Front foot flat, back foot on the ball
athlete_view.feel: Like a sprinter's lunge with a bar locked overhead.
key_actions: Front foot lands flat, slightly forward and slightly outside the hip
Back foot lands on the ball with a bent knee, slightly inside the hip line
Torso vertical, eyes forward, bar over the back of the head

coach_view.biomechanics: Split width is determined by lifter anthropometry but the principle is constant — the front shin should be roughly vertical (knee over ankle), and the back leg should be set so that the back hip extension angle approaches but does not reach maximum (~150-160 degrees of hip extension). The torso is vertical, with the bar's vertical line of force passing through the lifter's center of mass — between the feet.
priorities: Front shin vertical
Back foot on ball, knee bent
Vertical torso with locked overhead bar
coach_view.why_it_matters: An over-stretched split has no recovery. An under-stretched split fails to absorb the bar. The split is the lifter's stance — it has to be drilled until automatic.

common_errors.error: Front foot lands too far forward (over-stride)
common_errors.fix: Tape lines on the platform. Drill split walks. Cue 'short and tight, not long and loose.'

common_errors.error: Back foot flat (knee straight)
common_errors.fix: Train the split position statically. Lunge holds with locked-out load.

common_errors.error: Torso forward in the split
common_errors.fix: Vertical dip-drive emphasis. Behind-the-neck jerks reinforce vertical torso.

drills: Tall jerk (no dip — drop into split)
Static split holds with overhead load

split_recovery.title: Recovering from the Split
split_recovery.summary: Front foot steps back first (half-step), back foot steps forward to meet it. Bar held overhead throughout.

cues: Front foot back, then back foot up
athlete_view.feel: Controlled. The lift is over once the bar is overhead — recovery is just paperwork.
key_actions: Push slightly with the front leg to bring the front foot back ~half the split distance
Step the back foot forward to meet it
Hold until the down signal — three white lights

coach_view.biomechanics: Front-foot-first recovery is the standard because the front leg is the loaded leg — pushing back from a loaded position is more controlled than stepping forward from an unloaded back leg. Some lifters do a small front-foot adjustment then a back-foot step; some do a single decisive front-step. Either is acceptable in IWF rules as long as the bar stays overhead and the feet end on a line parallel to the platform's reference line.
priorities: Bar stable overhead during recovery
Feet end on a line
Hold to down signal
coach_view.why_it_matters: Lifters who 'recover' by stepping the back foot forward first often break overhead position because the load shifts unexpectedly. Front-foot-first is mechanically safer.

common_errors.error: Stepping back foot forward first
common_errors.fix: Drill the front-back sequence. Slow-motion recovery from split.

common_errors.error: Bar drifts during recovery
common_errors.fix: Static overhead holds. Strengthen overhead position.

common_errors.error: Dropping the bar before the down signal
common_errors.fix: Coach call: 'wait.' Train holding for 3-5 seconds in training.

alternatives.title: Squat Jerk and Power Jerk
alternatives.summary: The squat jerk catches the bar in a full overhead squat. The power jerk catches with feet stationary and a slight knee bend.

squat_jerk.title: Squat Jerk
squat_jerk.summary: Drive bar overhead and catch in a full overhead squat. No split.

cues: Bar over your head, you go down
athlete_view.feel: Like a snatch balance off the rack.
key_actions: Vertical drive, then drop into a full overhead squat
Stand up with the bar overhead

coach_view.biomechanics: The squat jerk requires elite overhead squat capacity, mobility, and overhead stability. It allows greater bar-receive height than the split (you can catch lower than you can split), so theoretically the strongest squat jerker can jerk slightly more than the strongest splitter. In practice, very few lifters squat-jerk because the recovery (standing from full overhead squat) is harder than recovering from a split. Notable exceptions: Lasha Talakhadze, some Chinese lifters.
priorities: Overhead squat strength must be world-class
Speed under the bar
Stable receive
coach_view.why_it_matters: Squat jerk is a specialist's tool — it works for lifters with exceptional overhead squat strength and mobility, and is often a poor choice for athletes with weaker overhead squats.

common_errors.error: Catching above parallel — defeats the point
common_errors.fix: Drop to full depth — cue 'sit in.'

common_errors.error: Failing to stand from depth
common_errors.fix: Overhead squat strength work.

power_jerk.title: Power Jerk
power_jerk.summary: Catch the bar with feet roughly in the dip-drive position, with a slight knee bend (above parallel).

athlete_view.feel: Like a heavy push press that you bend slightly to receive.
key_actions: Vertical drive
Quick re-bend of knees to receive bar overhead with feet stationary

coach_view.biomechanics: Power jerks are catch-above-parallel with no split. They require very high pull height (drive height) and are typically used for training the drive in isolation. Few lifters compete with power jerks at maximum loads — split or squat receives allow lower catch heights and therefore more weight.
priorities: Strong vertical drive
Quick foot reset / minor re-bend
coach_view.why_it_matters: Power jerks are excellent for testing/improving drive height. They are not the maxing tool — split jerk is.

common_errors.error: Splitting accidentally (foot moves backward)
common_errors.fix: Cue 'feet stay.' Drill from a standstill.

common_errors.error: Pressing out
common_errors.fix: Drive harder — the bar must reach lockout from leg drive alone.

drills: Push press into power jerk progression

programming.title: Programming and Periodization
programming.summary: How to organize snatch, clean & jerk, squat, pull, and accessory work over weeks, months, and macrocycles.

frequency.title: Training Frequency
frequency.summary: Most competitive lifters train 5-9 sessions per week with snatch and C&J variants in nearly every session.

cues: More frequency, less volume per session
Snatch and clean every training day
athlete_view.feel: Daily skill practice, not weekend-warrior training.
key_actions: Lift the classic lifts at least 4 days/week to build skill
Squat 3-4x/week
Pull variations 2-3x/week

coach_view.biomechanics: Olympic lifting is technique-dominant. Skill acquisition demands frequency. Most international programs touch the snatch and clean & jerk 4-6 times per week, often with multiple lifts per session. Volume per lift is moderate — 4-8 working sets at a given intensity is typical, not the 10+ sets common in bodybuilding.
priorities: Skill frequency over volume per session
Match frequency to recovery capacity
Beginners 3-4x/week, intermediates 4-5x/week, advanced 5-9x/week
coach_view.why_it_matters: Two snatch sessions per week produces two snatch repetitions worth of skill practice. Five sessions produces five — same load, more skill exposure. The Bulgarian (Abadjiev) extreme of 6+ daily sessions is the limit case; the Chinese model is similar but with more accessory work and slightly less classic-lift frequency.

common_errors.error: Treating Olympic lifts like powerlifting (1-2x/week heavy)
common_errors.fix: Add more sessions at lower per-session volume. Skill needs reps.

common_errors.error: Maxing every session as a beginner
common_errors.fix: Maxing belongs to lifters with 3+ years of consistent technique work. Beginners build with 70-85% intensities.

drills: See periodization models for structured frequency examples

intensity_zones.title: Intensity Zones

cues: 90%+ is for testing and peaking, not daily
athlete_view.feel: Most days are 'heavy work,' not 'crushing maxes.'
key_actions: Track percentages of current 1RM, adjust as PRs change
Use RPE if percentages don't match the day

coach_view.biomechanics: Optimal training intensity for skill development sits in the 70-90% range. Below 70%, technique often slips because the bar is too easy. Above 90%, technique strain accumulates and can grind down skill. Russian methodology (Medvedev, Roman) places ~60% of all classic-lift volume in the 70-89% zone.
priorities: Most volume at 70-89%
Strategic volume at 60-69% for technique
Limited volume above 90%
coach_view.why_it_matters: Lifters who only train above 90% burn out and stagnate. Lifters who only train below 70% never build the strength to handle real meet weights.

common_errors.error: Living above 90%
common_errors.fix: Wave the intensity. Heavy weeks at 85-95%, lighter weeks at 70-85%.

common_errors.error: Never going above 80% in training
common_errors.fix: Schedule heavy singles every 2-3 weeks. Test readiness with 90%+ openers in peak phases.

drills: See periodization models for week structures

periodization_models.title: Major Periodization Models
periodization_models.summary: The four most-used systems in Olympic weightlifting: Russian (block), Bulgarian (max-daily), Chinese (high-volume + accessories), Catalyst/American (linear-wave).

models.name: Russian / Soviet (Block Periodization — Medvedev / Roman)
models.structure: Macrocycle divided into preparation (volume), strength (intensity), and competition (peaking) phases. Each phase 3-6 weeks.
models.intensity_pattern: Preparation: 70-80% emphasis with high reps. Strength: 80-90% emphasis with lower reps. Peak: 90-100% with singles.
models.best_for: Athletes with 2+ years experience, structured training environment, periodic competitions.

models.name: Bulgarian (Abadjiev)
models.structure: Daily maxes on 3-5 exercises (snatch, C&J, front squat, back squat, sometimes power variants). Multiple sessions per day.
models.intensity_pattern: Max daily on each lift. No periodization waves.
models.best_for: Full-time professional lifters with elite recovery (medical support, full nutrition control). Catastrophic for amateurs.

models.name: Chinese System
models.structure: Two daily sessions, six days/week. Classic lifts in the morning, squats and accessories in the afternoon. Heavy accessory volume.
models.intensity_pattern: Daily heavy classic lifts (~90%) plus high accessory volume.
models.best_for: Professional environments. Adaptable to less elite athletes if accessory volume is reduced.

models.name: Catalyst / American (Greg Everett)
models.structure: 12-16 week macrocycles divided into 3-4 week mesocycles, each with a focus (volume → intensity → peak).
models.intensity_pattern: Linear or wave progression. Volume reduces, intensity increases over the macrocycle.
models.best_for: Most American competitive lifters. Compatible with regular work schedules.

models.name: Mash Method (Travis Mash)
models.structure: Concurrent-style with daily exposure to snatch, C&J, squat, and pull. RPE-based with planned heavy and light days.
models.intensity_pattern: Daily classic lift exposure with autoregulated intensity (RPE 7-9).
models.best_for: Intermediate to advanced lifters who respond well to autoregulation.

squat_programming.title: Squat Programming for Weightlifters
squat_programming.summary: Front squat and back squat 3-4x/week combined. Front squat 105-115% of clean; back squat 130-140% of clean is typical for elite.

cues: Front squat is more important than back squat for the C&J
Don't skip squats — they fund every lift
athlete_view.feel: Squats are the engine. Lifts are the steering.
key_actions: Squat 3-4 times/week (combined front + back)
Track squat ratios to your lifts — they reveal weak links

coach_view.biomechanics: Squat strength is the foundation of clean recovery and snatch stand. Front squat correlates more strongly with clean than back squat does. Pause squats build the deep catch position. Tempo squats build positional control. Olympic lifters typically squat 3-4 times/week with moderate volume per session (3-5 working sets).
priorities: Front squat 1RM ≥ 105% of clean 1RM (target 110-115%)
Back squat 1RM ≥ 125% of clean 1RM (target 135-140%)
Pause squats programmed regularly
coach_view.why_it_matters: A lifter whose front squat barely matches their clean has no margin in the catch. A lifter with deep front squat capacity catches with confidence.

common_errors.error: Squatting more than necessary at high RPE
common_errors.fix: Squat for the lifts, not as a sport. RPE 8 is the typical ceiling.

common_errors.error: Skipping front squats because back squat is easier
common_errors.fix: Front squat is the primary squat for weightlifting.

drills: Pause front squat (3 sec at bottom)

pulling_programming.title: Pulling Variations and Programming
pulling_programming.summary: Snatch pull and clean pull at 100-110% of classic lift, programmed 1-2x/week each. Deadlift variants for absolute strength.

cues: Match pull intensity to phase — heavier in strength block, lighter in peak
athlete_view.feel: Pulls feel heavy but controlled. They're not maxes.
key_actions: Snatch pulls 1-2x/week
Clean pulls 1-2x/week
Pull doubles or triples at 90-110% of lift

coach_view.biomechanics: Pulls overload the bar path and triple extension without the technique demands of the full lift. Snatch pull at 110% of snatch challenges first/second-pull mechanics. Snatch high pull and clean high pull add a 'shrug-and-elevate' component for turnover speed.
priorities: Pull intensity matches phase — accumulation 90-100%, intensification 100-110%
Pull volume 5-15 reps per session typical
Maintain technique under load — no compromised pulls
coach_view.why_it_matters: Pulls are the volume backbone of strength building for the classic lifts. They reinforce bar path under heavier-than-classic loads.

common_errors.error: Treating pulls as deadlifts (yanking from the floor)
common_errors.fix: Maintain classic-lift pull mechanics. If technique breaks down, reduce load.

common_errors.error: Pulling too heavy too often
common_errors.fix: Cycle pulls — heavy weeks at 105-110%, lighter weeks at 90-95%.

weekly_template_intermediate.title: Sample Weekly Template — Intermediate (4-Day)
weekly_template_intermediate.summary: A balanced 4-day template appropriate for an intermediate lifter (training age 1-3 years).

template.day_1: Snatch — work up to a heavy double at 80-85%. Snatch pull 3x3 @ 100-105%. Front squat 4x3 @ 80%. Accessory: ab work, back ext.
template.day_2: Clean & Jerk — work up to a heavy single complex (1+1) at 80-85%. Clean pull 3x3 @ 100-105%. Back squat 4x4 @ 75-80%. Accessory: pull-ups, RDLs.
template.day_3: Snatch variant (e.g. high-hang snatch or snatch from blocks) 5x2 @ 75%. Power snatch 4x2 @ 70%. Overhead squat 4x4 @ snatch+5%. Accessory: shoulder mobility.
template.day_4: Clean & Jerk variant (e.g. block clean or hang clean) 5x2 @ 75%. Power C&J 4x1 @ 80%. Front squat 4x4 @ 75%. Push press 4x4 @ jerk weight. Accessory: arms, core.

meet_prep.title: Meet Preparation and Competition Day
meet_prep.summary: Peaking, attempt selection, warmup-room timing, weigh-ins, and platform execution.

peaking_block_8_week.title: 8-Week Peak Structure
peaking_block_8_week.summary: Standard 8-week build to a meet. Volume drops, intensity rises, accessories taper.

structure.key_lifts: All classic lifts and variants, heavy accessory volume.
Classic lifts to 90%, pulls to 105-110%, squats to RPE 8.
Heavy singles weekly. Mostly classic lifts, pulls reduced. Reduce variants.
Open heavy. Last opener-style attempts midweek of week 2.
Light technique work early week. Last touch at 85-90% on Day -3 or -4. Rest day before meet.

cues: Last week is rest with light skill work
Don't chase PRs at the gym in the last 10 days
athlete_view.feel: Sharp by meet day. Heavy weights feel light because the system is rested.
key_actions: Plan the peak before week 8 starts
Adjust if illness or injury — never panic-add volume late

coach_view.biomechanics: Peaking is supercompensation. Volume reduction allows recovery; preserved intensity preserves the trained adaptations. Last 10 days are the most-violated phase by amateurs who 'just want to make sure they can hit the weight' — exactly the wrong instinct.
priorities: Reduce volume by 30-50% in the final week
Maintain intensity touches but with low volume
No new technical experiments in the final 4 weeks
coach_view.why_it_matters: Peaking is where intermediates separate from beginners. Beginners can't peak because their training base is too volatile. Intermediates can peak by 5-15% over their daily best.

final_week_protocol.title: Final Week Day-by-Day
final_week_protocol.summary: Standard taper week. Last touch 3-4 days before meet day. Day before is light or off.

days.session: Snatch to 85-90% single. Clean to 85-90% single. Jerk from rack 1-2 singles at 85%. Optional light squats.
Off OR light skill work (50-60% snatch and clean). Mobility.
Last 'touch.' Snatch to 90% single, clean and jerk to 90% single. No more. No squats.
Off OR very light bar work (40-50%). Walk, stretch, eat.
Off. Travel, equipment check, weigh-in if 24-hr-out, mental prep.

cues: Save the lifts for the platform
athlete_view.feel: Restless. That means the taper is working.
key_actions: Stick to the plan even if you feel great
Sleep 8+ hours per night the entire final week

coach_view.biomechanics: Performance peaking depends on supercompensation from accumulated training stress. The final week amplifies that recovery. Adding volume in the final week steals supercompensation from meet day.
priorities: Maintain neuromuscular sharpness with light/medium loads
Avoid creating new fatigue
Manage logistics so the lifter shows up rested
coach_view.why_it_matters: Athletes who train heavy in the final week consistently underperform their daily best by 5-10% on meet day. Athletes who taper consistently exceed their daily best by 5-10%.

weigh_in_protocols.title: Weigh-In Protocols
weigh_in_protocols.summary: IWF rules: weigh-in starts 2 hours before competition begins, lasts 1 hour. Attempts must be declared at weigh-in.
key_rules: Weigh-in window: 2 hours before competition session start, lasts 1 hour.
Lifters weigh in once. If overweight, they may re-weigh within the hour window.
Bodyweight is determined at weigh-in — no later check.
First snatch and first clean & jerk attempts must be declared at weigh-in (in writing on the official card).
Singlets, equipment must be checked and approved at weigh-in.
Lot number assigned at weigh-in determines lifting order in case of equal attempts.
Many local meets allow 24-hour or 2-hour weigh-ins — check the meet info packet.

cues: Bring your singlet, shoes, belt, and ID
athlete_view.feel: Calm. The hard work is done — this is paperwork.
key_actions: Arrive 30 minutes before weigh-in opens
Have backup snacks/fluids for after weigh-in
Submit openers — coach can change them later up until the call

coach_view.biomechanics: Cuts ending at weigh-in are followed by 2 hours of refeed before lifting. Aggressive water-cuts (>3% bodyweight in 24 hours) impair lifting performance. Most successful weight-class lifters cut <2% bodyweight via simple water/sodium manipulation.
priorities: Confirm weigh-in window with meet director
Plan refeed: easily-digested carbs, electrolytes, small meal
Have openers planned and committed before the day
coach_view.why_it_matters: Missing weigh-in is disqualification. Aggressive cuts can sabotage the meet that the entire training cycle was for.

attempt_selection.title: Attempt Selection Strategy
attempt_selection.summary: Opener: weight you'd hit on a bad day (typically 90-93% of training best). 2nd attempt: a stretch but realistic PR-zone. 3rd: PR or strategic attempt.

cues: An opener you miss is a disaster
Three white lights on the opener changes the meet
athlete_view.feel: Opener feels easy. 2nd feels like work. 3rd feels like a fight.
key_actions: Opener: ~90-93% of training best
3rd: 100-103% (or strategic — for a total or placing)

coach_view.biomechanics: Opening with a weight you've hit reliably in training in the last 4 weeks is the standard. Missing the opener is one of the most common ways athletes bomb out — they open at training-best weights they've only hit once or twice. Conservative openers preserve total. Strategic 3rd attempts target placement.
priorities: Opener weight that the lifter has hit at least twice in the last 6 weeks
Plan 2nd and 3rd in advance with multiple branches (made / missed scenarios)
Communicate with the warm-up room timer — coach watches attempts before yours
coach_view.why_it_matters: An athlete who goes 3-for-3 on conservative weights almost always out-totals an athlete who goes 1-for-3 on aggressive weights, regardless of training maxes.

common_errors.error: Opening at the lifter's training best
common_errors.fix: Drop opener 5-10kg. Aggressive openers cause bombs.

common_errors.error: Jumping too much between attempts
common_errors.fix: Standard jumps are 3-5kg for snatch, 3-7kg for C&J. Larger jumps require strong rationale.

common_errors.error: No plan for missed attempts
common_errors.fix: Decide before the meet: if I miss the opener, what do I take next? Repeat or drop?

drills: Mock-meet sessions in training: declare openers, hit them cold, no warmup-room benefits

warmup_room_timing.title: Warm-Up Room Timing
warmup_room_timing.summary: A coach times the lifter's warmups so the last warmup lift is taken just before the lifter is called to the platform.

cues: Take what the coach says — no extras
athlete_view.feel: Controlled tension. Not flat, not pre-fatigued.
key_actions: Warmup room arrival 45-60 minutes before estimated first attempt
Coach calls each warmup weight; lifter executes
Last warmup is 5-10kg below opener

coach_view.biomechanics: Olympic meets typically run ~1 minute per attempt in the early rounds and 1.5-2 minutes per attempt later (more clock time per lifter as fewer remain). The coach counts attempts ahead of the lifter and times warmups so the last warmup happens 1-3 attempts before the lifter is called. A standard snatch warmup is 7-9 lifts; clean & jerk is 6-8 lifts.
priorities: Count attempts ahead — know how many lifters take how many attempts before yours
Coach manages warmup tempo, not the lifter
Final warmup 3-5kg below opener for a confident touch
coach_view.why_it_matters: A lifter who's warmed up too early goes flat by the time they hit the platform. Too late, and they're under-prepared. Timing is the coach's most operational job on meet day.

common_errors.error: Lifter directing their own warmup
common_errors.fix: Coach controls. Lifter executes.

common_errors.error: Going to warmup room too early
common_errors.fix: Stay in the back, eat, hydrate. Move to warmup room when 8-10 attempts away.

drills: Mock-meet warmup with a coach calling weights and timing

platform_execution.title: Platform Execution
platform_execution.summary: 1 minute on the clock from name call (2 minutes for back-to-back lifts by the same lifter). Three white lights = good lift.
key_rules: 1-minute clock starts when the lifter's name is called.
2-minute clock if the lifter takes consecutive attempts (2nd or 3rd attempt and they were just called previously).
Lifter must wait for the down signal before lowering the bar.
Three judges: at least 2 white lights = good lift; 2+ red lights = no lift.
No press-out (visible elbow re-bend during lockout).
Bar must end on the platform under control (not dropped from above shoulder height before lifter is established overhead).
Feet must end on a line parallel to the bar in the jerk recovery.

cues: Eyes on the bar, then on the platform
Take the lift you trained for
athlete_view.feel: Tunnel vision. The crowd disappears.
key_actions: Step to the bar, set, lift
Hold the bar until the down signal
Walk off the platform without celebrating until you see the lights

coach_view.biomechanics: The 1-minute clock is rarely a factor for prepared lifters but can pressure tired or anxious lifters. Coaches in the warmup room confirm the call and the time, then the lifter is the only person on the platform.
priorities: Lifter executes the lift they trained
No press-outs
Hold to down signal
coach_view.why_it_matters: Most platform errors are emotional, not technical. Lifters miss because they hurry, because they second-guess, or because they get distracted by the crowd or the previous attempt.

common_errors.error: Lowering the bar before the down signal
common_errors.fix: Drill 'wait for lights' in training. Hold every overhead for 2-3 seconds after standing.

common_errors.error: Press-out on a snatch or jerk
common_errors.fix: Pre-meet: more aggressive triple extension and faster lockout. Pre-platform: visualize fast lockout.

common_errors.error: Stepping out of the lifting area before the bar is grounded
common_errors.fix: Set the bar down inside the platform area first, then exit.

weight_class_management.title: Weight Class Management
weight_class_management.summary: Cutting under 2% via water/sodium manipulation is the standard; larger cuts (>3%) hurt performance.

cues: Don't cut more than 2-3% in the last week
athlete_view.feel: Cuts should feel manageable. If you're suffering, you're cutting too much.
key_actions: Plan target bodyweight 8 weeks out
Cut gradually via diet, not dehydration
Last 24-72 hours: water/sodium taper if needed

coach_view.biomechanics: Each kg of cut weight is roughly equivalent to 0.5-1.5kg lost off snatch and 0.7-2kg lost off C&J — depending on the lifter and the cut speed. Cutting weight is rarely worth it unless the gain in placement (different category) outweighs the performance loss. Modern IWF body categories were redesigned in 2018 — confirm current categories per the meet's rule set.
priorities: Plan cut over weeks, not days
Maintain protein intake during the cut
Refeed properly between weigh-in and lifting
coach_view.why_it_matters: Lifters who cut hard often bomb. Lifters who lift in their walking-around weight class often outperform their fellow strugglers.

common_errors.error: Last-minute aggressive water cut
common_errors.fix: Plan ahead. If you can't make weight cleanly, lift in the next class up.

common_errors.error: No refeed strategy after weigh-in
common_errors.fix: Have a refeed plan: easy carbs, electrolytes, light protein, ready 5-10 minutes after weigh-in.

bombing_out_prevention.title: Bombing Out Prevention
bombing_out_prevention.summary: Bombing = missing all three attempts in either lift. The single most-feared and most-preventable meet outcome.

cues: If you miss the opener, drop the next attempt
Get one good lift on the board no matter what
athlete_view.feel: If the opener feels heavy, the rest of the day is going to be a fight. Adjust.
key_actions: Opener at 90-93% of training best
If opener missed, repeat or drop 1-3kg — never go up
Get on the board first, total later

coach_view.biomechanics: Bombing is almost always caused by aggressive openers, not by surprise weakness. The exception is illness, injury, or weight-cut depletion. Most bomb-outs trace back to openers within 2-3% of training maxes — a margin too tight for meet-day variability.
priorities: Conservative opener
Pre-planned response to a missed opener
Mental rehearsal of recovery
coach_view.why_it_matters: Bombing wastes a training cycle. A 1-for-3 day with a small total still produces meet experience and ranking points. A 0-for-3 day produces zero.

common_errors.error: Aggressive opener to make a target total
common_errors.fix: The total is built from three made lifts, not three attempted lifts. Open conservative.

common_errors.error: Going up after a missed attempt
common_errors.fix: Repeat or drop. Going up after a miss is a recipe for a second miss.

supportive_equipment.title: Supportive Equipment (IWF-Legal Gear)
supportive_equipment.summary: Olympic weightlifting permits supportive gear but does not allow 'equipped' lifting in the powerlifting sense. No suits, shirts, or wraps that store elastic energy.

lifting_shoes.title: Olympic Lifting Shoes
lifting_shoes.summary: Raised heel (typically 0.5-1 inch / 13-25mm), rigid sole, secure midfoot strap. Standard equipment for all serious training.

cues: Heel height matched to ankle mobility
athlete_view.feel: Stable. Like the ground was raised under your heel.
key_actions: Pick a heel height that allows full squat depth without forward torso lean
Replace shoes every 3-5 years for serious lifters — soles compress over time

coach_view.biomechanics: Raised heel reduces required ankle dorsiflexion in the squat, allowing more upright torso. Rigid sole prevents energy loss into shoe compression. Common heel heights: 0.5" (Adidas Powerlift, lower-mobility lifters), 0.75" (Romaleos middle), 1.0" (Romaleos high heel, longer-femur lifters).
priorities: Heel height matched to athlete anthropometry and mobility
Stiff sole — no running shoe compression
Secure midfoot lockdown
coach_view.why_it_matters: Lifting in flat-soled shoes (or running shoes) is a measurable performance loss for nearly all lifters and is unnecessary risk for ankles.

common_errors.error: Lifting in cross-trainers or runners
common_errors.fix: Get lifting shoes. Adidas Powerlift, Romaleos, Reebok Legacy, Nike Romaleos are all standard.

common_errors.error: Heel too high for body proportions
common_errors.fix: Try lower heel options — not all lifters benefit from 1" heels.

belt_use.title: Belt Use
belt_use.summary: IWF rules: belt max 12cm wide, leather or synthetic. Worn outside the singlet. Used most commonly above 80% intensity.

cues: Brace into the belt, not relying on the belt
Remove for warmups, accessories, light work
athlete_view.feel: Tight but not uncomfortable. You should breathe into it.
key_actions: Belt at 80%+ on classics
Belt for heavy squats (typically 85%+)
Don't belt for technique work, light lifts, or accessories

coach_view.biomechanics: The belt provides a tactile reference for trunk bracing and increases intra-abdominal pressure by ~10-15%. It does not replace bracing — it amplifies it. Lifters who become belt-dependent often have weaker unaided trunk strength and fail under unbelted conditions.
priorities: Belt is a tool, not a crutch
Maintain unbelted strength via lower-intensity work
Belt position: tight at the waist, just below the ribs
coach_view.why_it_matters: Belts add a measurable but small performance increase (~3-5% on max squats and pulls). Over-reliance creates a bracing skill deficit.

common_errors.error: Wearing the belt for everything including warmups
common_errors.fix: Save belt for top sets. Build trunk strength unbelted.

common_errors.error: Wearing belt too loose or too tight
common_errors.fix: Belt should be tight enough to prevent rotation but loose enough to permit a deep belly breath.

knee_sleeves_vs_wraps.title: Knee Sleeves vs Knee Wraps
knee_sleeves_vs_wraps.summary: IWF allows knee sleeves (max 30cm length, single-layer construction). Knee wraps are NOT permitted in IWF Olympic weightlifting competition.

cues: Sleeves for warmth and proprioception, not for support
Replace sleeves when they lose elasticity
athlete_view.feel: Snug warmth. Sleeves should feel supportive but not tight enough to restrict the squat.
key_actions: Use sleeves at 80%+ classic lifts and squats
Choose 5-7mm thickness for general use
Confirm meet-specific equipment rules — some federations differ from IWF

coach_view.biomechanics: Knee sleeves provide warmth (vascular benefit), light compression (proprioceptive feedback), and minor support — but no significant elastic-energy storage. Compression-style sleeves (e.g. SBD 7mm) approach the rule limit but remain legal. Wraps store and release elastic energy and provide significant lifting assistance — they are banned in IWF for that reason.
priorities: Confirm meet rules — IWF, USAW, and local federations may differ
Use sleeves consistently in training so the meet day matches
Inspect sleeves for tears or stretching
coach_view.why_it_matters: Showing up to a meet with wraps and being told they're not allowed creates last-minute equipment problems. Confirm before traveling.

common_errors.error: Wearing wraps in IWF/USAW meets
common_errors.fix: Wraps are not permitted. Use sleeves only.

common_errors.error: Sleeves too thin (no support) or too thick (illegal)
common_errors.fix: 5-7mm is standard. Confirm thickness against current IWF technical regulations.

wrist_wraps.title: Wrist Wraps
wrist_wraps.summary: Permitted. Standard for most lifters above 80% intensity. Provides wrist support during overhead and front-rack positions.

cues: Wraps around the wrist joint, not too high up the forearm
Tight enough to support, loose enough to allow full extension
athlete_view.feel: Like a brace at the wrist.
key_actions: Wrap diagonally, anchor on the back of the hand
Loosen between sets to maintain blood flow

coach_view.biomechanics: Wrist wraps reduce passive wrist extension demand during overhead lifts and front rack positions. Especially useful for lifters with limited wrist mobility or for high-volume sessions where wrist fatigue accumulates.
priorities: Tightness matched to need — heavier work = tighter wrap
Don't compensate for poor mobility with permanent reliance
coach_view.why_it_matters: Wraps prevent wrist injury during overhead sessions, particularly in lifters who haven't built passive wrist tolerance.

common_errors.error: Wrapping too high (forearm)
common_errors.fix: Wrap at the wrist joint.

common_errors.error: Lifting all sessions in wraps
common_errors.fix: Train light work without wraps to build joint tolerance.

singlet_and_attire.title: Singlet and Attire Rules
singlet_and_attire.summary: IWF requires a singlet covering torso. T-shirts allowed under singlet. Knee-length socks allowed but not concealing the knee.
key_rules: Singlet (one-piece) required for IWF competition.
T-shirts permitted under or as part of attire (must not have collar covering the neck).
Socks allowed up to mid-calf (must not cover the knee, which would conflict with knee sleeve rules).
Tape on hands, fingers, wrists, knees permitted but cannot extend onto the bar contact area.
Headwear/hair: no specific rules but must not interfere with judge visibility.
Local meets may relax these — confirm meet packet.

cues: Practice in meet-day attire ahead of time
athlete_view.feel: Free range of motion. Nothing binds at the shoulders or hips.
key_actions: Buy singlet 2+ months before meet, train in it 2-3 sessions to confirm fit
Backup singlet recommended

tape_use.title: Tape Use (Hands and Fingers)
tape_use.summary: Tape on thumbs and fingers is standard for managing skin tolerance with the hook grip. IWF rules allow tape but not on the bar-contact surface of the hand.

cues: Tape thumbs for hook grip skin tolerance
Don't tape over the palm where the bar sits
Practice with the same tape in training
athlete_view.feel: Tape protects the thumb skin under the hook grip squeeze.
key_actions: Pre-cut strips before warmup
Test tape brand/style in training before meet day

coach_view.biomechanics: The hook grip pinches the thumb under the bar, causing skin damage if the thumb is unconditioned. Tape distributes pressure and prevents the thumb from splitting. Standard tape: 1-inch athletic tape or specific pre-wrap product.
priorities: Tape on thumb (always for hook grip), optionally on fingers for grip wear
Tape brand and method consistent with training
coach_view.why_it_matters: A torn thumb on meet day means lifting with un-hooked grip, which means a snatch or clean failure on heavy attempts.

accessory_work.title: Accessory Work
accessory_work.summary: Supplemental movements that build the strength qualities and tissue tolerance the classic lifts require.

back_strength.title: Back Strength (Erectors and Lats)
back_strength.summary: Back-extension and lat-engagement work supports first-pull mechanics and overhead position.

exercises.name: Snatch grip RDL
exercises.purpose: First-pull positioning, hamstring length under load

cues: Back strength is what holds positions under heavy loads
Volume here is high — sets of 6-10 typical
athlete_view.feel: Pumpy, tiring. Not heart-rate spiking but locally fatiguing.
key_actions: Program 2-4 back accessory sets per session
Cycle exercises every 6-8 weeks

coach_view.biomechanics: Back strength (erectors, lats, mid-back) is the often-cited weak link in amateur lifters. The lats specifically are the limiting factor in bar-path control during the first pull. Back extension volume builds erector tolerance for pulling.
priorities: 2-4 back-focused exercises per week
Sets of 6-12 reps for volume
Pause variations for positional strength

leg_strength.title: Leg Strength Beyond the Squat
leg_strength.summary: Bulgarian split squats, lunges, step-ups, and unilateral work for leg strength balance and joint resilience.

cues: Squats build the engine, accessories build resilience

coach_view.biomechanics: Asymmetries in the lower body are common in lifters and can manifest as bar path deviations under heavy load. Unilateral work addresses these. Volume should be moderate — accessories don't need to feel like main work.
priorities: 1-2 unilateral exercises per week
Volume in the 8-15 rep range

pressing_and_overhead.title: Pressing and Overhead Strength
pressing_and_overhead.summary: Press, push press, push jerk, behind-the-neck press for overhead strength and lockout.

exercises.name: Strict overhead press
exercises.purpose: Pure shoulder strength, overhead position support

exercises.name: Sots press
exercises.purpose: Active end-range overhead mobility under load

cues: Press supports the jerk and the overhead
Don't neglect strict press for the sake of fancier movements

coach_view.biomechanics: Strict press strength correlates with overhead stability and jerk lockout. Push press translates directly to jerk drive mechanics. Behind-the-neck pressing builds the snatch overhead position more directly than front pressing.
priorities: Press 2x/week minimum
Mix strict and dynamic variants

core_and_trunk.title: Core and Trunk Work
core_and_trunk.summary: Anti-extension, anti-rotation, and bracing-specific work supports every lift.

cues: A weak trunk leaks force everywhere
5-10 minutes of trunk work per session pays off long-term

coach_view.biomechanics: Trunk weakness shows up as bar path deviations under heavy load (lifter buckles forward, sideways, or backward). Programming 5-15 minutes of trunk work per session is standard.
priorities: Anti-extension primary
Anti-rotation secondary
Heavy carries weekly

mobility_and_recovery.title: Mobility and Recovery
mobility_and_recovery.summary: Position-specific mobility (overhead, ankle, hip) and recovery practices that allow high-frequency training.

overhead_mobility.title: Overhead Mobility
overhead_mobility.summary: Shoulder flexion, external rotation, and thoracic extension support a stable overhead receive position.

ankle_mobility.title: Ankle Mobility
ankle_mobility.summary: Dorsiflexion supports squat depth without compensatory forward lean.

drills.name: Knee-to-wall dorsiflexion test
drills.purpose: Diagnostic — 4-5 inches knee-to-wall is common goal

hip_mobility.title: Hip Mobility
hip_mobility.summary: Hip flexion, internal rotation, and adduction support deep squat positions.

recovery_practices.title: Recovery Practices
recovery_practices.summary: Sleep, nutrition, and programming load management are the dominant recovery levers. Modalities are secondary.

athlete_view.feel: Recovery is a non-negotiable, not a bonus.
key_actions: Track sleep and bodyweight as recovery indicators
Schedule deloads in the program — don't wait for breakdown

coach_view.biomechanics: Recovery is dominated by sleep, nutrition, and programming load — in that order. Modalities (massage, sauna, contrast bath, ice baths) provide marginal returns. The single most overlooked recovery factor is total weekly training stress relative to fitness.
priorities: Sleep first
Nutrition second
Programming load third
Modalities fourth
coach_view.why_it_matters: Lifters who chase fancy recovery while sleeping 6 hours and undereating get inferior results to lifters who sleep 8 hours and eat properly with no extra modalities.

psychology_and_mental_game.title: Psychology and Mental Game
psychology_and_mental_game.summary: Pre-lift routines, attempt commitment, and competition-day arousal management.

pre_lift_routine.title: Pre-Lift Routine
pre_lift_routine.summary: A short, repeatable routine before every attempt — same in training as in meets.

cues: Same routine, every lift, every time
No second-guessing once you approach the bar
athlete_view.feel: Automatic. The body knows what to do.
key_actions: Develop a 5-10 second pre-lift routine in training
Use the same sequence in warmups and on the platform
Examples: chalk hands, set feet, breath in, brief visualization, lift

coach_view.biomechanics: A pre-lift routine reduces decision-making load on meet day. Athletes with consistent routines perform more consistently under pressure. The routine should be short (5-10 seconds), physical (not just mental), and developed in training so that meet-day pressure doesn't disrupt it.
priorities: Develop routine in training, not meet day
Keep it short and physical
Trust it under pressure

arousal_management.title: Arousal Management on Meet Day
arousal_management.summary: Optimal performance lives in a moderate arousal zone — too low and the lifter is flat, too high and the lifter loses fine motor control.

athlete_view.feel: Like a focused workout, not an emergency.
key_actions: Avoid stimulants in excess (over-caffeinating throws fine motor)
Use breathing to settle if too high
Use music or coach prompts to amp up if too flat

coach_view.biomechanics: Performance follows an inverted-U curve with arousal. Olympic lifting requires precision (high skill) under heavy load — too much arousal degrades motor coordination. Caffeine, music, crowd noise, and self-talk all influence arousal. Most amateur lifters err on the high-arousal side; experienced lifters learn to dial it down.
priorities: Calibrate caffeine intake from training
Have arousal-up and arousal-down tools ready
Trust automatic execution

missed_lift_response.title: Responding to a Missed Lift
missed_lift_response.summary: Reset routine, decide weight with coach, lift the next attempt without dwelling.

cues: Lift the next one — that's the only one that matters
athlete_view.feel: Disappointed, then refocused. Quick turnaround.
key_actions: Walk off platform without showing emotion
Coach handles weight selection
Re-enter the warmup mindset

coach_view.biomechanics: Athletes who dwell on missed attempts often miss the follow-up attempt as well. Coach role: reset the athlete physically (breathing, posture, brief technical cue) and decide the next attempt strategy. Athlete role: trust and execute.
priorities: No dwelling
Coach decides, athlete executes
Get back to routine
